title: Hooked rug geometric border
subtitle: A simple step-and-chevron border pattern for a hooked rug on hessian
excerpt: Learn to plan and hook a geometric step-and-chevron border for a small hooked rug. This entry covers marking the border pattern onto hessian, cutting fabric strips, and hooking in rows to produce a clean, even geometric design around the perimeter of a rug.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional rug-hooking practice. Geometric border designs are one of the most common patterns in traditional British and North American hooked rug traditions; the step-and-chevron is a classic documented in Beatrice Harvey, Rug Hooking (1950s, in-copyright, substance only referenced).

glossaryTerms.definition: The process of pulling loops of fabric strip or yarn up through a hessian backing using a hook tool. Each pulled loop sits at the same height above the backing to produce an even pile surface.
The open-weave jute backing fabric used as the foundation for hooked rugs. The open weave allows the hook to pass between the threads to pull loops through from the reverse side.

The width of fabric strips used as the hooking material. For a geometric border, a consistent cut width of 5 to 7 mm produces defined, readable shapes without the detail being lost in the loop height.

content.text: A geometric border is the most rewarding first pattern for anyone who has learned the basic
action: the pattern is defined entirely by colour changes along clean horizontal and diagonal lines. The
grid acts as built-in graph paper. A consistent
(6 mm for a traditional-scale border) gives neat, solid shapes.

items.name: Hessian rug backing, at least 50 x 50 cm
Wool fabric strips, ground colour (centre field)

content.text: Step 1: Mark the pattern on the hessian

Mark the outer rug boundary 5 cm from each edge (for finishing allowance). Mark the inner boundary of the border 8 to 10 cm inside the outer line. The central field and the border surround it. Using a permanent marker and a ruler, draw the step-and-chevron pattern in the border zone: a series of stepped chevrons 3 to 4 threads wide, each step rising or falling by 3 threads across 6 threads of width.

Mount the hessian on the rug frame. Begin by hooking the dark colour along the marked chevron lines, following the lines exactly. Each loop should be pulled to a height of about 6 mm above the backing. Work row by row, hooking outlines before filling areas.
Step 3: Fill the border and the field

Fill between the dark chevron outlines with the light colour, hooking in the same direction as the outlines where possible. Fill the inner field with the ground colour, working in curved rows following the shape of the rug rather than in straight lines: curved rows produce a more even surface than grid-straight rows in the centre of a rug.

When hooking is complete, trim all loop heights to an even level from the right side using scissors if any loops have pulled higher than the rest. Bind the edges with cotton rug binding tape, folding the hessian allowance onto the back before sewing down. Press under a damp cloth on the reverse side.

items.problem: The chevron steps look ragged rather than crisp
items.cause: The marked lines were not followed precisely enough when hooking
items.fix: Hook exactly on the marked line, placing each loop in the hessian hole closest to the marking. Count threads carefully at each step change.

items.problem: The rug buckles and does not lie flat
items.cause: The hooking density is uneven or the backing is held too loosely on the frame
items.fix: Steam the rug on the reverse side with a damp cloth and press firmly. The steam relaxes the hessian threads and reduces buckling.
